
Imagine Catalyx wants to build a satellite.

A normal software project has:

```
Frontend
Backend
Database
```

A satellite has:

```
Software
Electronics
Battery
Sensors
Communication System
Thermal System
Mechanical Structure
Ground Station
```

Many teams work together.

Now suppose someone writes:

`"The satellite shall transmit data every 10 seconds."`

This requirement is stored in a PDF.

Months later:

- Software team interprets it one way.
- Electronics team another way.
- Testing team another way.

Chaos.

---

### Idea

Create a **model**.

Think of it like Google Maps.


#### What is a Model?

Suppose we're building a drone.

Instead of writing:

```
Drone contains:
- Battery
- Motor
- GPS
```

We draw:
<svg version="1.1" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 424.99697200992705 372.1712873098659" width="424.99697200992705" height="372.1712873098659"><!-- svg-source:excalidraw --><metadata></metadata><defs><style class="style-fonts">

28.76 20.47, 50 0" stroke="#e03131" stroke-width="2" fill="none"></path></g><g transform="translate(188.5019410416562 282.78605044981305) rotate(0 14.879989624023438 10)"><text x="14.879989624023438" y="14.096" font-family="Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji" font-size="16px" fill="#e03131" text-anchor="middle" style="white-space: pre;" direction="ltr" dominant-baseline="alphabetic">Gps</text></g></svg>

Something like that

### What is SysML? (System modeling language)

>SysML → Language for describing systems


so it is a language just like we need vs code and other editor called tools to write it we need a **tool**

to draw models 

***so let's look at some Industry standard tech stacks***


---


An MBSE product is not just a diagramming tool.

A complete MBSE platform typically needs:



<svg version="1.1" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 792.1600036621094 569.5989086894551" width="792.1600036621094" height="569.5989086894551"><!-- svg-source:excalidraw --><metadata></metadata><defs><style class="style-fonts">
